cities.name: Kolkata
cities.overview: Kolkata is the cultural capital of India — a city of literature, art, politics, and festivity. Built on the eastern bank of the Hooghly River, it was the capital of British India until 1911, leaving behind grand Victorian architecture, tram lines, and a legacy that shaped modern India. Home to the warmest street food culture and the most passionate Durga Puja celebrations in the world.
cities.history: Founded by the British East India Company in 1690 at the village of Kalikata, Kolkata (then Calcutta) grew into the seat of British power in India. It was the birthplace of the Bengal Renaissance — a cultural and intellectual awakening that produced figures like Rabindranath Tagore, Raja Ram Mohan Roy, and Swami Vivekananda. India's first Nobel laureate, first metro railway (1984), and first stock exchange all belong to Kolkata.

nearest_airport.name: Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose International Airport

nearest_railhead.name: Howrah Junction / Sealdah
nearest_railhead.note: Two of India's busiest railway stations within the city

places.name: Victoria Memorial
places.description: A magnificent white marble monument built between 1906 and 1921 in memory of Queen Victoria. Set in lush 64-acre gardens on the banks of the Hooghly, it now houses a museum with priceless colonial-era artefacts, paintings, and manuscripts. The most iconic landmark of Kolkata.
places.tips: Visit the sound and light show in the evenings (Oct–Mar). The gardens are lovely at dusk. Book tickets online to skip queues.

places.name: Howrah Bridge (Rabindra Setu)
places.description: One of the world's busiest cantilever bridges, spanning the Hooghly River. Built in 1943 without a single nut or bolt — held entirely by rivets — it carries over 100,000 vehicles daily. The flower market (Mullick Ghat) at its base is a fragrant spectacle at dawn.
places.tips: Walk across the bridge at sunrise for stunning river views and the flower market below. Avoid during rush hour by vehicle.

places.name: Dakshineswar Kali Temple
places.description: A 19th-century Bengali-style temple complex dedicated to Goddess Kali on the northern bank of the Hooghly. Intimately connected to Sri Ramakrishna Paramahamsa, who served as priest here and had his divine visions in this very spot.
places.tips: Visit early morning (6–8am) to avoid heavy crowds and witness the morning aarti. Cross the Bali Bridge for the best exterior view of the temple.

places.name: College Street & Coffee House
places.description: The world's largest second-hand book market — miles of pavement stalls selling rare Bengali and English texts. The 1876 Indian Coffee House is the legendary hub where generations of Kolkata's intellectuals, poets, freedom fighters, and filmmakers have debated over strong coffee.
places.tips: Go on a weekday morning for the best browsing. The Coffee House is always crowded — go for the atmosphere, not the service speed.

places.name: Kumartuli (Potters' Quarter)
places.description: The ancient neighbourhood where artisans sculpt thousands of clay idols of Durga, Saraswati, and Kali for festivals. Watching a master sculptor shape a goddess from Ganges clay and straw is one of Kolkata's most moving experiences.
places.tips: Visit September–October to see Durga idol construction at its peak. The narrow lanes are best explored on foot. Photography is welcomed by most artisans.

places.name: Park Street
places.description: Kolkata's most famous dining and entertainment street — lined with historic restaurants, jazz bars, bookshops, and cafes that have defined the city's cosmopolitan identity since the 1960s. Mocambo, Peter Cat, and Flurys are legendary institutions here.
places.tips: Go for dinner — the street is electric at night. Try chicken chelo kebab at Peter Cat. Flurys bakery at breakfast is a must.

places.name: Belur Math
places.description: The global headquarters of the Ramakrishna Mission, founded by Swami Vivekananda in 1899. The architectural masterpiece of the main temple blends Hindu, Buddhist, Muslim, and Christian elements in its design — a stone metaphor for the unity of all religions.
places.tips: Cross the Hooghly to visit both Belur Math and Dakshineswar on the same day. Silence is observed within the main temple complex.

cities.name: Darjeeling
cities.overview: Darjeeling perches in the eastern Himalayas with a sweeping view of the Kanchenjunga massif — the world's third-highest mountain. Renowned for its muscatel tea, the UNESCO-listed Himalayan Railway toy train, colonial architecture, and one of India's most dramatic sunrises at Tiger Hill. A truly timeless mountain town.
cities.history: Darjeeling was leased by the British from the Chogyal of Sikkim in 1835 and developed as a sanatorium and summer retreat. Tea plantations were introduced in the 1840s by Dr. Campbell, creating the world-famous Darjeeling tea industry. The Darjeeling Himalayan Railway (1881), a UNESCO World Heritage Site, winds 88km up from New Jalpaiguri through some of the most dramatic scenery in South Asia.

nearest_railhead.name: New Jalpaiguri (NJP)
nearest_railhead.note: Then toy train or taxi to Darjeeling

places.name: Tiger Hill Sunrise
places.description: At 2,590m, Tiger Hill is the highest point near Darjeeling and the best place to watch the sun rise over Kanchenjunga (8,586m) and the Himalayan range. On clear winter mornings, the silhouette of Everest is also visible far to the northwest.
places.tips: Book a jeep the night before — shared cabs leave around 3:30am. December–February gives the clearest views. Carry warm layers.

places.name: Darjeeling Himalayan Railway (Toy Train)
places.description: A UNESCO World Heritage railway built in 1881. The 2ft narrow-gauge steam locomotive loops and reverses its way up 88km from the plains to Darjeeling through tea gardens, waterfalls, and mountain curves. The 2-hour joy ride from Darjeeling to Ghum and back is one of India's most beloved experiences.
places.tips: Book the joy ride at Darjeeling station in the morning. The Ghum station is the highest railway station in India at 2,258m.

places.name: Happy Valley Tea Estate
places.description: One of Darjeeling's oldest tea estates — a working garden where you can walk through rows of tea bushes clinging to misty hillsides, see the plucking process, tour the factory, and taste freshly produced first-flush Darjeeling tea.
places.tips: Go during first flush (March–April) to see and taste the most prized tea. Factory tour is included in the entry fee.

places.name: Batasia Loop & War Memorial
places.description: A spectacular spiral railway loop where the toy train makes a full 360° turn to manage the steep gradient. A Gurkha War Memorial sits at the centre of the loop. The view of Kanchenjunga from here is superb on clear mornings.
places.tips: Watch the toy train pass through the loop — it comes around twice a day. Combine with the war memorial visit.

places.name: Padmaja Naidu Himalayan Zoological Park
places.description: The world's highest-altitude zoo, home to the Snow Leopard, Red Panda, Tibetan Wolf, and Clouded Leopard. Famous for its successful Snow Leopard and Red Panda breeding programmes. Set amidst pine forest with Kanchenjunga visible in the background.
places.tips: The Red Panda enclosure is unmissable. Go early in the morning when animals are most active.

places.name: Observatory Hill (Mahakal Temple)
places.description: The sacred hilltop at the centre of Darjeeling, revered by Hindus and Buddhists alike, draped in thousands of prayer flags. The Mahakal Shiva temple at the summit has a commanding view of the town and mountains.
places.tips: Visit at dawn or dusk for the most atmospheric experience. The prayer flag colours against the mountain backdrop are extraordinary.

cities.name: Sundarbans
cities.tagline: Land of the Royal Bengal Tiger
cities.overview: The Sundarbans is the world's largest mangrove forest and a UNESCO World Heritage Site, straddling India and Bangladesh. It is the only mangrove forest in the world inhabited by the Royal Bengal Tiger. A labyrinth of tidal rivers, mud flats, and dense mangroves — navigable only by boat — the Sundarbans is one of the most extraordinary ecosystems on Earth.
cities.history: The Sundarbans has been inhabited since ancient times by the Moli (honey collectors) and fishermen communities who navigated its rivers under the protection of Bonbibi, the forest goddess. The British East India Company began systematic documentation in the 18th century. The Tiger Reserve was established in 1973 under Project Tiger, one of India's first. The ecosystem supports over 260 bird species and 42 mammal species.

nearest_airport.name: Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose International Airport, Kolkata

places.name: Sundarbans Tiger Reserve (Boat Safari)
places.description: A boat safari through the dense mangrove waterways of the world's largest tiger reserve. Spot Royal Bengal Tigers on mudflats at low tide, estuarine crocodiles, Irrawaddy dolphins, spotted deer, and wild boar. The eerie silence of the mangrove is as powerful as any sighting.
places.tips: Book a package through West Bengal Tourism — individual entry is difficult. Tiger sightings are never guaranteed. Carry binoculars and a telephoto lens.

places.name: Sajnekhali Watch Tower & Bird Sanctuary
places.description: The main entry point and interpretation centre of the Sundarbans Tiger Reserve. A watch tower overlooking a creek where spotted deer, monitor lizards, and birds gather. The Bonbibi temple here is sacred to the local fishing community.
places.tips: This is always the first stop on a Sundarbans tour. The museum gives good context before the forest safari.

places.name: Dobanki Canopy Walk
places.description: A 600m elevated canopy walkway through the mangrove forest — one of the few places where you can walk inside the Sundarbans above the waterline. A freshwater pond at the end attracts spotted deer, wild boar, and water birds.
places.tips: Maintain absolute silence — animals are easily spooked. Early morning visits (8am) give the best chance of deer and bird sightings.

cities.name: Shantiniketan
cities.overview: Shantiniketan is the creation of Rabindranath Tagore, who founded Visva-Bharati University here in 1921 — now a Central University and UNESCO World Heritage Site (2023). An open-air campus of murmuring trees, terracotta architecture, and outdoor classrooms, it remains India's most uniquely humanistic seat of learning and culture.
cities.history: Debendranath Tagore, Rabindranath's father, established a meditation ashram here in 1863. Rabindranath transformed it into an experimental open-air school in 1901 that blended Indian and Western educational ideals. The university became a magnet for India's greatest artists, musicians, and thinkers. Poush Mela (December), a fair of folk music, dance, and crafts, has been held here since 1894.

nearest_railhead.name: Bolpur
nearest_railhead.note: Direct trains from Howrah and Sealdah daily

places.name: Visva-Bharati University Campus
places.description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site (2023) — the unique open-air university campus created by Tagore with classrooms under trees, terracotta-decorated buildings, and the famous Uttarayan complex where Tagore lived and wrote. The Sangit Bhavana (music faculty) and Kala Bhavana (fine arts) are world-class.
places.tips: Hire a local guide from the tourism office for the campus walk. Photography restrictions apply inside some buildings.

places.name: Rabindra Bhavana Museum
places.description: A museum preserving Tagore's manuscripts, personal belongings, photographs, paintings, and original letters. The collection traces the life and creative evolution of the Nobel laureate — essential for any visitor with even a passing interest in Tagore.
places.tips: Buy the official illustrated catalogue at the gift shop — it is the best souvenir from Shantiniketan.

places.name: Poush Mela (Fair)
places.description: A 130-year-old winter fair held every December — a stunning confluence of Baul folk music, tribal dance, handicrafts, terracotta, kantha embroidery, weaves, and open-air performances that draw visitors from across the world.
places.tips: Book accommodation months in advance for the Poush Mela — Bolpur fills up entirely. Arrive early each day for best access to stalls.

cities.name: Kalimpong
cities.overview: Kalimpong is a quieter, less-touristy hill town compared to Darjeeling, set at the confluence of three mountain ranges and overlooking the Teesta River. Renowned for its flower nurseries (especially orchids and gladioli), Buddhist monasteries, and a legacy as an old Silk Road trading town between India, Bhutan, and Tibet.
cities.history: Kalimpong was part of Bhutan until 1865, when it was transferred to British India after the Anglo-Bhutan war. It thrived as a trade hub on the route from India to Tibet through the Jelep La pass. The town became a centre for Christian missionaries who established schools still considered among Bengal's finest. Novelist Rumer Godden and freedom activist Lal Bahadur Shastri have connections to the town.

nearest_railhead.name: New Jalpaiguri (NJP)
nearest_railhead.note: Taxi to Kalimpong along the Teesta River (2.5 hrs)

places.name: Deolo Hill
places.description: The highest point near Kalimpong at 1,704m, offering panoramic views of Kanchenjunga, the Teesta River Valley, and the plains of Siliguri. A beautifully maintained park with a children's area, making it popular with families.
places.tips: Visit on clear winter mornings for Kanchenjunga views. The park is peaceful in the early morning before crowds arrive.

places.name: Zang Dhok Palri Phodang Monastery
places.description: A Nyingma-tradition monastery housing a rare collection of Tibetan Buddhist texts and thangkas. The monastery was consecrated by the Dalai Lama and is known for its collection of over 60,000 rare Buddhist manuscripts.
places.tips: This is one of Kalimpong's most spiritually significant sites. Visitors are welcome but should maintain silence inside.

places.name: Kalimpong Flower Nurseries
places.description: Kalimpong supplies 80% of West Bengal's cut flowers including gladioli, orchids, carnations, and birds-of-paradise. Several nurseries welcome visitors for tours — walking through a professional orchid nursery is a unique and fragrant experience.
places.tips: Udai Vilas Nursery and Nursery Cactus are popular. The November–January blooming season is the most spectacular.

cities.name: Murshidabad
cities.overview: Murshidabad was once the wealthiest city in the world — the seat of the Nawabs of Bengal before the Battle of Plassey (1757) that transferred power to the British East India Company. Its grand palaces, mosques, and riverside ghats survive as a testament to Bengal's richest era of royal patronage.
cities.history: Nawab Murshid Quli Khan moved the Bengal capital here from Dhaka in 1717, naming it after himself. The city rivalled London in wealth in the early 18th century. The Battle of Plassey in 1757 — fought 50km away — ended Nawab Siraj-ud-Daullah's reign and marked the beginning of British dominance over India. Murshidabad still contains the finest concentration of Mughal-era architecture in Bengal.

nearest_airport.name: Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose International Airport, Kolkata

places.name: Hazarduari Palace
places.description: The 'Palace of a Thousand Doors' — a 19th-century Italian Baroque palace built by Nawab Nazim Nazir Ali in 1837. Its 1,000 doors (114 false), mirrored Durbar Hall, collection of palanquins, portraits of Nawabs, and Siraj-ud-Daullah's sword make it the crown jewel of Murshidabad.
places.tips: Arrive by 10am before tour groups arrive. The museum collection on the upper floors is remarkably well-preserved.

places.name: Katra Mosque & Murshid Quli Khan's Tomb
places.description: The early 18th-century mosque built by Nawab Murshid Quli Khan, who is buried beneath the entrance staircase by his own wish — so every worshipper would walk over his grave in humility. An extraordinary, haunting piece of Islamic architecture.
places.tips: Non-Muslim visitors are welcome in the outer courtyard. The story of the tomb's location is one of Bengal's most extraordinary historical anecdotes.

places.name: Plassey Battlefield
places.description: The site of the 1757 Battle of Plassey where Clive's 3,000 troops defeated Nawab Siraj-ud-Daullah's 50,000-strong army — one of history's most consequential battles, which gave Britain control of Bengal and eventually all of India. A small memorial marks the site today.
places.tips: The site is historically significant but physically unimpressive — visit for its historical resonance, not its aesthetics. Combine with Murshidabad town.

cities.name: Bishnupur
cities.overview: Bishnupur was the capital of the Malla dynasty for centuries and is home to some of India's finest terracotta temples — intricate brick and clay sculptures depicting scenes from the Mahabharata, Ramayana, and Krishna's life. It is also the birthplace of the Bishnupuri gharana of classical Indian music and the famous Bishnupur silk sarees.
cities.history: The Malla kings ruled Bishnupur from the 7th century, eventually converting to Vaishnavism and commissioning a series of magnificent terracotta temples from the 17th–18th centuries. The unique architectural style blends Bengali hut forms with terracotta relief panels of extraordinary detail. The Bishnupuri style of Hindustani classical music traces its lineage to this court.

nearest_airport.name: Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose International Airport, Kolkata

nearest_railhead.name: Bishnupur
nearest_railhead.note: Direct trains from Howrah, approximately 3.5 hours

places.name: Rasmancha
places.description: Built in 1600 by King Hambir Malla — one of the oldest brick temples in Bengal. A unique pyramidal structure with 108 arched verandahs used for the annual Ras festival where Krishna idols were brought from different temples to be displayed together.
places.tips: This is the grandest and most photogenic of Bishnupur's temples. Go in morning light — the terracotta glows warmly against the sky.

places.name: Jorbangla & Mrinmoyee Temples
places.description: The Jorbangla temple (1655) is the finest example of the double-hut terracotta style — its exterior panels are covered with scenes from the Ramayana, Mahabharata, and Krishna Leela carved in exquisite detail. The oldest temple in Bishnupur, Mrinmoyee, dates back to the 10th century.
places.tips: Take a local guide to explain the stories depicted on the terracotta panels — many panels tell complete narratives that are easy to miss.

places.name: Dalmadal Cannon
places.description: A 17th-century cannon of legendary proportions — 4 metres long and cast from a single piece of metal by the Malla kings. According to legend, the cannon fired by itself (by divine intervention) to repel a Maratha invasion. One of the most celebrated artefacts in Bengal's history.
places.tips: The cannon is open to the elements in a central square — easy to visit in passing between temples.

cities.name: Siliguri
cities.overview: Siliguri is not a destination in itself but the essential gateway to five states — Sikkim, Darjeeling, Kalimpong, Assam, and Bhutan. Known as the 'Chicken's Neck', it is the narrow corridor connecting Northeast India to the rest of the country. It is a bustling, energetic transit hub with excellent food and shopping.
cities.history: Siliguri developed as a trading and transit town under British rule due to its strategic position at the foot of the Himalayas. The establishment of the Darjeeling Himalayan Railway terminus at New Jalpaiguri (adjacent to Siliguri) cemented its role as the entry point to the eastern Himalayas. Today it is the commercial capital of North Bengal.

nearest_railhead.name: New Jalpaiguri (NJP)
nearest_railhead.note: Major hub for trains to entire Northeast India

places.name: Hong Kong Market
places.description: A bustling bazaar known for inexpensive electronics, shoes, garments, and accessories. Named after Hong Kong goods that used to be smuggled through this border region, the market remains one of North Bengal's liveliest commercial hubs.
places.tips: Bargaining is expected. Early evening is the best time to visit when the market is fully alive.

places.name: Salugara Monastery
places.description: A serene Tibetan Buddhist monastery with a striking 100-foot stupa surrounded by prayer wheels. Monks from Bhutan, Sikkim, and Nepal study here. The monastery complex is an oasis of calm near the busy highway.
places.tips: Spin the large prayer wheels at the base of the stupa — a deeply meditative ritual. Morning prayers at 5am are open to visitors.

cities.name: Cooch Behar
cities.tagline: The Royal City of North Bengal
cities.overview: Cooch Behar is a royal town near the Bhutan border with a stunning Italianate palace, ancient temples, and a legacy as the capital of the Koch dynasty that once ruled much of Northeast India. Quiet and unhurried, it is one of Bengal's best-kept heritage secrets.
cities.history: The Koch Kingdom was one of the most powerful dynasties of medieval Bengal and Assam, ruling from the 16th century. Their capital at Cooch Behar was later transformed by Maharaja Nripendra Narayan in the 19th century into a model of European town planning with a grand palace, parks, and broad roads. The state merged with India in 1949.

nearest_railhead.name: New Cooch Behar
nearest_railhead.note: Regular trains from Kolkata (NJP junction for faster connections)

places.name: Cooch Behar Palace
places.description: A magnificent Indo-European palace modelled on Buckingham Palace and built in 1887 by Maharaja Nripendra Narayan. It has a grand central dome and is set in well-maintained gardens. Now a protected monument under ASI with a museum of royal artefacts inside.
places.tips: The museum on the ground floor has remarkable royal portraits, weapons, and personal objects of the Koch Maharajas. Well-maintained and rarely crowded.

places.name: Madan Mohan Temple
places.description: The principal temple and religious heart of Cooch Behar, dedicated to Lord Madan Mohan (Krishna). Built by the Koch kings, it is surrounded by smaller temples and a sacred tank. The Ras festival here is one of the grandest in North Bengal.
places.tips: Visit during the Ras Purnima festival (November) when the town transforms with fairs and processions.

cities.name: Digha
cities.overview: Digha is Bengal's most popular beach resort — a long stretch of firm golden sand on the Bay of Bengal that draws millions of Bengali visitors each year, especially families and honeymooners. The beach is wide, gently sloping, and safe for wading. Mandarmani and Shankarpur nearby offer quieter alternatives.
cities.history: Digha was discovered by Warren Hastings, the first Governor-General of India, who called it 'the Brighton of the East' in the 18th century. It remained a quiet fishing village until the 1920s when a road was built from Calcutta. The Digha–Shankarpur Development Authority was established in 1976, transforming it into West Bengal's premier coastal resort.

nearest_airport.name: Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose International Airport, Kolkata

nearest_railhead.name: Digha
nearest_railhead.note: Direct trains from Howrah, approximately 3 hours (Tamralipta Express)

places.name: Old Digha Beach
places.description: The main and widest beach stretch at Digha — a long crescent of firm sand on the Bay of Bengal. Best enjoyed at sunrise before the crowds arrive, or at sunset when the sky turns vivid orange over the sea.
places.tips: Sunrise walks (5:30–7am) are magical and uncrowded. Avoid swimming deep into the sea — currents can be strong. Tidal horses (pony rides) are available all day.

places.name: Mandarmani Beach
places.description: A quieter, less-developed beach 20km from Digha, known for its long unbroken stretch of sand perfect for driving along the shoreline. Red crabs appear on the beach in large numbers in the early morning — a remarkable natural spectacle.
places.tips: Walk the beach at low tide at dawn to see thousands of red ghost crabs emerging from the sand. Far less crowded than Digha on weekdays.

places.name: Marine Aquarium & Research Centre
places.description: A state aquarium and research centre with tanks displaying the marine life of the Bay of Bengal — seahorses, octopus, sharks, and local fish species. Modest by global standards but interesting for children and families.
places.tips: Best for families with children. Combine with a walk on the beach before or after. Feeding time is usually 3pm.

cities.name: Purulia
cities.overview: Purulia is Bengal's westernmost district — a rugged, red-laterite landscape of Sal forests, rocky hills, and tribal villages bordering Jharkhand. Home to the Santhal and Munda peoples, it is the birthplace of the Chhau martial dance — a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage. Ajodhya Hills and Pakhi Pahar offer spectacular scenic beauty unlike anywhere else in Bengal.
cities.history: Purulia was historically part of the Jungle Mahal — a region of dense forests and tribal kingdoms that resisted Mughal and British control for centuries. The Chhau dance tradition evolved from warrior training practices of the local chieftains. Purulia has one of the highest concentrations of indigenous Santhal, Munda, and Ho tribes in West Bengal.

nearest_airport.name: Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose International Airport, Kolkata

nearest_railhead.name: Purulia
nearest_railhead.note: Direct trains from Howrah, approximately 5–6 hours

places.name: Ajodhya Hills (Matha)
places.description: A forested plateau rising to 700m in the Purulia highlands — a landscape of granite boulders, Sal and Mahua forests, waterfalls, and tribal villages. The Upper and Lower Dams at Ajodhya provide stunning sunset views over still water amid the hills.
places.tips: Hire a local jeep from Purulia for the hills — the road is rough. The Turga Waterfall is spectacular in September just after monsoon.

places.name: Chhau Dance Performance
places.description: Purulia Chhau is a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage — a vibrant masked dance form depicting battles from the Ramayana, Mahabharata, and Puranas. Performed with elaborate masks, acrobatic martial movements, and live percussion. The Chaitra Parva festival in April is the grandest occasion.
places.tips: Contact the Chhau Dance Academy in Charida village for performance schedules. The Chaitra Parva festival (March–April) is the best time to witness it authentically.

places.name: Charida — Mask Maker's Village
places.description: An entire village where nearly every household is involved in making the elaborate papier-mâché Chhau dance masks. A visit reveals the multi-step process of mould-making, painting, and decorating — and masks can be bought directly from the artisans.
places.tips: Masks cost ₹200–₹5,000 depending on size and complexity. Buying directly from artisans supports the community and gives fair prices.

cities.name: Gangasagar (Sagar Island)
cities.tagline: Where the Ganga Meets the Sea
cities.overview: Gangasagar is the sacred island at the confluence of the Hooghly River and the Bay of Bengal — one of Hinduism's most important pilgrimage sites. Every January during Makar Sankranti, over a million pilgrims arrive to bathe in the holy waters. Beyond the sacred, it is also the edge of the Sundarbans with a wild, windswept beach.
cities.history: Gangasagar is mentioned in the Mahabharata and Puranas as the site where Sage Kapila's ashram stood and where the sons of King Sagar were reduced to ashes. The Kapil Muni Temple on the island commemorates this legend. The annual Ganga Sagar Mela is one of India's largest pilgrimage gatherings — second only to the Kumbh Mela in scale.

nearest_airport.name: Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose International Airport, Kolkata

nearest_railhead.name: Sealdah → Namkhana
nearest_railhead.note: Train to Namkhana, then ferry to Sagar Island (1 hour)

places.name: Kapil Muni Temple
places.description: The ancient temple dedicated to Sage Kapila at the very tip of Sagar Island, right where the Hooghly meets the Bay of Bengal. Pilgrims take a holy dip in the sea immediately in front of the temple — the most sacred ritual of the Ganga Sagar Mela.
places.tips: Visit very early morning (before 7am) for the most peaceful experience. The Makar Sankranti festival (January 14–15) draws over a million pilgrims.

places.name: Sagar Island Beach
places.description: A wild, wind-swept beach at the southern tip of Sagar Island — the frontier between the Ganges and the ocean. Cassuarina trees line the shore. Dolphins are occasionally sighted offshore. The light here at dusk is remarkably beautiful.
places.tips: Swimming is not safe — currents are extremely strong. Walk the beach at dusk for the most spectacular light over the Bay of Bengal.
